verses.commentary: They approach Jerusalem, come to Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives. Final approach. Jesus sends two disciples. Deliberate preparation for the entry.

Go to the village. You will find a colt tied, which no one has ever ridden. Untie it, bring it here. Detailed instructions. Supernatural knowledge or prearrangement? Either way, sovereign orchestration.
If anyone asks, say: The Lord needs it and will send it back shortly. The Lord (Kyrios, Κύριος). Jesus claims authority over property. Owner complies.
They find the colt exactly as Jesus said. Outside in the street, tied at a doorway.
People ask: What are you doing? Reasonable question.
They answer as Jesus instructed. People let them go. The word suffices.
They bring the colt, throw cloaks over it. Jesus sits on it. Zechariah 9:9 fulfilled: your king comes, gentle, riding on a donkey. Messianic entry.
People spread cloaks on the road, others spread branches. Royal treatment. 2 Kings 9:13 echoes: Jehu proclaimed king. Jesus receives kingly honor.
They shout: Hosanna (Save now)! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Psalm 118:25–26. Pilgrimage psalm becomes messianic acclamation.
Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David! Hosanna in the highest! Davidic kingdom proclaimed. Political overtones. Expectations of liberation from Rome.

Jesus enters Jerusalem, goes to the temple courts. He looks around at everything. Inspection. But since it's late, he goes to Bethany with the Twelve. Calm before the storm. He observes, then withdraws.
Next day leaving Bethany, Jesus is hungry. Human need.
He sees a fig tree in leaf, goes to find fruit. Nothing but leaves, because it was not the season for figs. Strange detail. Why curse a tree out of season? Symbolic act coming.

May no one ever eat fruit from you again. Curse. Disciples hear. Fig tree = Israel (Hosea 9:10, Jeremiah 8:13, Micah 7:1). Leaves without fruit = appearance without substance. Judgment on Israel's fruitlessness.
Jesus enters temple courts, drives out buyers and sellers. Overturns money changers' tables, benches of dove sellers. Violent prophetic action. Cleanses the temple.
He stops anyone from carrying merchandise through the temple courts. Ends commercial traffic. The house of prayer has become a marketplace.

He teaches: My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations (Isaiah 56:7). But you have made it a den of robbers (Jeremiah 7:11). Two indictments: exclusion of Gentiles, exploitation. The temple has failed its purpose.
Chief priests and teachers of the law hear, look for a way to kill him. They fear him because the crowd is amazed at his teaching. Authority threatens their power. Murder plot begins.
Evening comes, Jesus and disciples go out of the city. Strategic withdrawal.
Morning. They see the fig tree withered from the roots. Complete, rapid death. Curse fulfilled.
Peter remembers: Rabbi, look! The fig tree you cursed has withered! Astonishment.
Have faith in God. Jesus' response. The lesson is not about fig trees but about faith.

Truly, if anyone says to this mountain, Go, throw yourself into the sea, and does not doubt but believes, it will be done. This mountain likely the Temple Mount. Faith can remove mountains. Hyperbolic but serious. The temple system itself can be removed by faith.
Whatever you ask in prayer, believe you have received it, and it will be yours. Radical promise. Faith prays with confidence.

When you stand praying, if you hold anything against anyone, forgive them, so your Father in heaven may forgive your sins. Forgiveness is prerequisite for answered prayer. Vertical relationship depends on horizontal relationships.
Verse missing in most manuscripts. Later addition: But if you do not forgive, neither will your Father forgive your sins.
They arrive in Jerusalem. Jesus walks in temple courts. Chief priests, teachers, elders come. Official delegation. Sanhedrin represented.
By what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you authority? Challenge. They demand credentials. Jesus disrupted their system; they want justification.
Jesus: I will ask you one question. Answer me, and I will tell you by what authority. Counter-question. Rabbinic technique.
John's baptism, was it from heaven or of human origin? Tell me! Brilliant trap. John prepared the way for Jesus. Their answer to John determines their answer to Jesus.
They discuss: If we say from heaven, he will ask why we didn't believe him. Dilemma. Admitting John's divine authority exposes their rejection.
But if we say of human origin. They fear the people, for everyone held John was a prophet. Political calculation. Truth subordinated to expediency.

They answer: We don't know. Cowardice. Jesus: Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things. Their refusal to acknowledge truth disqualifies them from receiving more. The chapter sandwiches the temple cleansing between fig tree cursing and withering. Israel, like the fig tree, has leaves (religious activity) but no fruit (righteousness). Judgment is coming. Jesus enters Jerusalem as king, judges the temple, and provokes the authorities. The final confrontation begins.
